nativeRange.habitat: The Four Stripe Damselfish inhabits sheltered lagoons, harbors, inlets, and inshore reef areas throughout its range, often found near small coral heads and Acropora outcrops. It has been recorded at depths from 1 to 68 meters.

diet.notes: Accepts a variety of meaty foods, herbivore preparations, and flaked/pellet foods. In the wild feeds on larval shrimps, crabs, algae, ostracods, amphipods, copepods, pelagic tunicates, and fish eggs.

imageCandidates.notes: Photographer: Rickard Zerpe; taken near Rao west of Morotai, Indonesia, June 2019. High-resolution macro photograph. Subject unambiguously identified as Dascyllus melanurus in the Commons file title.
Photographer: Bos, A.R. (Philippines). FishBase species photo; copyright retained by photographer — contact contributor for permission before use.
Product hero image from LiveAquaria listing for Four Stripe Damselfish (SKU 70067). Clean composition typical of retailer product photography.

summary: The Four Stripe Damselfish is a bold black-and-white striped species from the Western Pacific, widely considered one of the hardier saltwater fish available to beginners. It is generally regarded as reef-safe but tends to become territorial and aggressive toward tankmates as it matures.
careNotes: The Four Stripe Damselfish (Dascyllus melanurus) is broadly considered one of the more resilient saltwater fish, and was historically used by hobbyists to establish the nitrogen cycle in new marine tanks — a practice now widely discouraged in favor of fishless cycling methods. Its temperament as an adult means it is best added to a community reef tank last, after other inhabitants have settled, or kept alongside other assertive species that can hold their own. It will claim and fiercely defend a territory, often centered on a coral head or rock formation. Juveniles can be kept in small groups, but adults tend to become increasingly intolerant of one another and of smaller, more passive tankmates. The species is generally regarded as reef-safe and will not harm invertebrates or disturb aquascaping. In the wild, juveniles are commonly found sheltering near Acropora coral heads in lagoons and inshore reef areas throughout the Western Pacific.
breedingNotes: Oviparous with distinct pair bonding during breeding season. Eggs are demersal, adhesive, and attached to substrate. Males guard and aerate the eggs until hatching. Captive breeding is uncommon in home aquaria but has been documented in larger systems.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (confirmed ~8 cm), native range, habitat, diet composition, and water temperature. Temperature range 24.9–29°C per FishBase vs 22.2–25.6°C per LiveAquaria — minor disagreement; FishBase value used as scientific authority.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (family Pomacentridae, order Blenniiformes), common names, native range, temperament, and diet. Consistent with FishBase and LiveAquaria.

fish.breedingNotes: The Four Stripe Damselfish is an egg-layer that forms distinct pair bonds during the breeding season. Eggs are deposited on a hard surface (demersal spawning), and the male guards and aerates the clutch until hatching. Breeding in home aquaria is uncommon and considered difficult; it has been achieved in larger, species-specific setups.
